title: Proton transfer reactions
summary: This lesson introduces the Brønsted-Lowry theory of acids and bases, focusing on proton transfer. You will learn to identify acids, bases, and their conjugate pairs, understand the behaviour of amphiprotic species, and appreciate the crucial role of the solvent in these reactions.
learningObjectives: Define acids and bases according to the Brønsted-Lowry theory.
Identify conjugate acid-base pairs in proton transfer reactions.
Describe and provide examples of amphiprotic species.
Explain how the solvent can influence the acidic or basic character of a substance.

simpleExplanation.title: The Proton Dance
simpleExplanation.summary: In chemistry, an acid is a molecule that gives away a proton (an H⁺ ion), and a base is a molecule that accepts it. This simple transfer is one of the most fundamental reactions in chemistry.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a dance where partners are constantly being exchanged. An acid is like a dancer who arrives with a partner (a proton) and gives them to a base, who arrived alone and was looking for a partner. After the swap, the acid is now alone (becoming a 'conjugate base') and the base has a new partner (becoming a 'conjugate acid').
steps: Identify the reactants: Look at the starting materials and determine which species will donate a proton (the acid) and which will accept it (the base).
Visualise the transfer: Show the movement of a single H⁺ ion from the acid to the base. Remember, only the proton moves, not any other atoms.
Form the products: Write the chemical formula for what the acid becomes after losing H⁺, and what the base becomes after gaining H⁺.
Link the conjugate pairs: Connect the original acid to its product (the conjugate base) and the original base to its product (the conjugate acid). Each pair differs by only one H⁺.

flashcards.back: A species that donates a proton (an H⁺ ion).
A species that accepts a proton (an H⁺ ion).

flashcards.front: What is a conjugate acid-base pair?
flashcards.back: Two species that differ from each other by a single proton (H⁺). For example, HCl (acid) and Cl⁻ (conjugate base).
The species formed when a Brønsted-Lowry base accepts a proton.
The species formed when a Brønsted-Lowry acid donates a proton.
A species that can act as either a Brønsted-Lowry acid (donating a proton) or a Brønsted-Lowry base (accepting a proton).

flashcards.front: Give an example of an amphiprotic species.
flashcards.back: Water (H₂O) can donate a proton to become OH⁻, or accept a proton to become H₃O⁺. The hydrogencarbonate ion (HCO₃⁻) is another common example.

flashcards.front: What is the conjugate base of sulfuric acid, H₂SO₄?
flashcards.back: The hydrogensulfate ion, HSO₄⁻. They differ by one H⁺.

flashcards.front: What is the conjugate acid of ammonia, NH₃?
flashcards.back: The ammonium ion, NH₄⁺. They differ by one H⁺.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between amphiprotic and amphoteric?
flashcards.back: Amphiprotic specifically describes the ability to donate/accept a proton (Brønsted-Lowry). Amphoteric is a broader term for any species that can act as an acid or a base (including Lewis acids/bases). All amphiprotic species are amphoteric, but not all amphoteric species are amphiprotic.

flashcards.front: In the reaction $CH_3COOH + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CH_3COO^- + H_3O^+$, which species is the Brønsted-Lowry base?
flashcards.back: H₂O, because it accepts a proton from ethanoic acid to form the hydronium ion, H₃O⁺.

flashcards.front: What is a 'proton' in the context of acid-base chemistry?
flashcards.back: It refers to a hydrogen ion, H⁺, which is a hydrogen atom that has lost its electron.

faq.q: What is the difference between amphiprotic and amphoteric?
faq.a: Amphiprotic is a specific term within the Brønsted-Lowry theory. It means a species can both donate and accept a proton (H⁺). Amphoteric is a more general term. It means a substance can act as an acid or a base, but doesn't specify the mechanism. For example, aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃) is amphoteric because it reacts with both acids and bases, but it is not amphiprotic because it cannot donate a proton. All amphiprotic species are amphoteric, but not all amphoteric species are amphiprotic.

faq.q: Does a free H⁺ proton actually exist in solution?
faq.a: No, not for any significant amount of time. A proton (H⁺) is a bare nucleus with a very high charge density, making it extremely reactive. In an aqueous solution, it is immediately solvated by a water molecule, forming the hydronium ion, H₃O⁺. While we often write H⁺(aq) for simplicity, it's crucial to understand that it always exists as a more complex species like H₃O⁺.

sections.content: While you may have previously learned that acids produce H⁺ ions and bases produce OH⁻ ions in water, this definition is limited. The Brønsted-Lowry theory provides a more powerful and general framework for understanding a vast range of chemical reactions, not just those in aqueous solution. It redefines acids and bases based on a single, fundamental process: the transfer of a proton.

In 1923, Johannes Brønsted and Thomas Lowry independently proposed a new set of definitions. A Brønsted-Lowry acid is a proton donor, and a Brønsted-Lowry base is a proton acceptor. A 'proton' in this context is simply a hydrogen ion (H⁺). This definition elegantly describes acid-base behaviour as a competition for protons.

items: An acid must contain a hydrogen atom that it can lose as an H⁺ ion.
A base must have a lone pair of electrons to form a new bond with an H⁺ ion.
Acid-base reactions are proton transfer reactions.
The reaction is often reversible, leading to an equilibrium.

sections.content: Every time a Brønsted-Lowry acid donates a proton, it forms its conjugate base. Similarly, when a base accepts a proton, it forms its conjugate acid. A conjugate acid-base pair therefore consists of two species that are interconverted by the transfer of a proton. They always differ by exactly one H⁺.

sections.question: For the following equilibrium, identify the Brønsted-Lowry acid, base, conjugate acid, and conjugate base: $$\text{HNO}_2(\text{aq}) + \text{CN}^-(\text{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \text{NO}_2^-(\text{aq}) + \text{HCN}(\text{aq})$$
sections.solution:
1. **Identify the acid and base**: Compare the reactants (left side) with the products (right side). The nitrous acid, HNO₂, loses a proton (H⁺) to become the nitrite ion, NO₂⁻. Therefore, HNO₂ is the acid. The cyanide ion, CN⁻, gains a proton to become hydrogen cyanide, HCN. Therefore, CN⁻ is the base. [1 mark]

2. **Identify the conjugate acid and base**: The species formed after the acid (HNO₂) donates its proton is the conjugate base (NO₂⁻). The species formed after the base (CN⁻) accepts a proton is the conjugate acid (HCN). [1 mark]

3. **State the pairs**: 
   - Acid: HNO₂
   - Base: CN⁻
   - Conjugate Base: NO₂⁻
   - Conjugate Acid: HCN

   The conjugate pairs are (HNO₂ / NO₂⁻) and (HCN / CN⁻). [1 mark for both pairs correct]

sections.content: A strong acid readily donates its proton, meaning its conjugate base has a very weak attraction for protons and is therefore a very weak base. Conversely, a weak acid has a strong conjugate base. This inverse relationship is a key concept for predicting the direction of acid-base equilibria.

Some substances have the remarkable ability to act as either an acid or a base depending on the chemical environment. A species that can both donate and accept a proton is called **amphiprotic**. Water is the most common example: it can donate a proton to form OH⁻ or accept a proton to form H₃O⁺. The term **amphoteric** is more general and refers to any substance that can act as an acid or a base, including under definitions other than Brønsted-Lowry (like Lewis theory). All amphiprotic species are amphoteric, but the reverse is not always true.

sections.question: The dihydrogen phosphate ion, H₂PO₄⁻, is amphiprotic. Write two separate equations to show its behaviour, one acting as a Brønsted-Lowry acid and one as a Brønsted-Lowry base, both with water.
sections.solution:
1. **Acting as an acid**: When H₂PO₄⁻ acts as an acid, it donates a proton (H⁺) to a base. Water will act as the base.
   Equation: $$\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-(\text{aq}) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l}) \rightleftharpoons \text{HPO}_4^{2-}(\text{aq}) + \text{H}_3\text{O}^+(\text{aq})$$ 
   Here, H₂PO₄⁻ donates a proton to become its conjugate base, HPO₄²⁻. [1 mark for correct equation]

2. **Acting as a base**: When H₂PO₄⁻ acts as a base, it accepts a proton (H⁺) from an acid. Water will act as the acid.
   Equation: $$\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-(\text{aq}) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l}) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4(\text{aq}) + \text{OH}^-(\text{aq})$$ 
   Here, H₂PO₄⁻ accepts a proton to become its conjugate acid, H₃PO₄. [1 mark for correct equation]

*Note: In both cases, state symbols are important for full marks in an exam.*

sections.content: The Brønsted-Lowry theory highlights that 'acidity' is not an inherent property of a substance but depends on its interaction with another species, often the solvent. A substance might be an acid in one solvent but a base in another. For example, ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH) is a weak acid in water, donating a proton to H₂O. However, if dissolved in a much stronger acid like anhydrous sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄), it is forced to act as a base and accept a proton.
In this reaction, sulfuric acid is the stronger acid, so it donates the proton, and ethanoic acid acts as the base.
